title: Reading Input
description: Make your apps interactive by reading user strings.

slides.title: Talking back 🗣️
slides.content:
## Interactive Programs

Until now, our programs simply ran their course and finished. They couldn't react to us.

To make an interactive app, we need to ask the user a question, and pause to wait for their typed answer.

slides.title: readln() 🛑
slides.content:
We use the `readln()` function to read input from the console. 

When Kotlin hits this line, the program **pauses** and waits for the user to type something and press Enter/Return. It then returns that text as a `String`.
slides.codeSnippet:
println("What is your name?")
val userName = readln()

println("Nice to meet you, $userName!")

quizData.question: What data type does `readln()` always return, regardless of what the user typed?
quizData.explanation: Text from the console is always captured as a String, even if the user just typed numbers.

slides.title: Converting Input 🔢
slides.content:
If the user types "25", `readln()` still returns the **text string** `"25"`, not the **number** `25`.

You cannot do math with a String. You must convert it to an Int using `.toInt()`.
slides.codeSnippet:
println("Enter your age:")
val ageString = readln()

// Convert the string to a real integer
val age = ageString.toInt()

println("In 10 years, you will be ${age + 10}.")

quizData.question: What method do you use to convert a String like "500" into an actual Integer?
quizData.explanation: The `.toInt()` method attempts to convert the String to an Int.
